segments.text:
Macrophage (purple), monocyte, (blue,) bind and phagocytose bacteria
D.  Innate Immunity

Innate Immunity
Defenses against infection that are ready prior to attack by a pathogen
Consists of anatomical barriers, chemical, and cellular responses

Adaptive or Acquired Immunity
Induced by exposure to antigens, includes B and T cells

Immune system
Innate			Adaptive

keratinocytes
Anatomical Barriers: Skin
Psoriasin is secreted by keratinocytes in skin

Psoriasis-skin disease characterized by the infiltration of neutrophils
Psoriasin 11.5 kd

Psioriasin
-it is synthesized by keratinocytes, especially in response to wounds
-directly interacts/kills bacteria
-detected in other  hyper-proliferative and inflammatory skin diseases including psoriasis
-activates neutrophils to secrete cytokines and chemokines
-induces the expression and release of defensens
Mucous in the digestive system

Epithelial cells line internal surfaces
Goblet Cell

Structure of Mucin
The amino- and carboxy-terminal regions are very lightly glycosylated, but rich in cysteines which participate in establishing disulfide linkages within and among mucin monomers.
A large central region formed of multiple tandem repeats of 10 to 80 residue sequences in which up to half of the amino acids are serine or threonine. This area becomes saturated with hundreds of O-linked oligosaccharides.
Humans have many genes that encode mucins
Different mucins for different jobs
Mucociliary escalator is part of the innate immune system

Mucin hyperproduction
is associated with the
disease Cystic Fibrosis
Lysozyme is secreted by neutrophils, macrophages and submucosal glands
Submucosal glands

Lysozyme digests the cell wall of
Gram positive bacterial cells
This polysaccharide is very tough, crystal violet sticks well

Anatomical
Barriers

Defensins
-synthesized by granulocytes
-cationic- Bind to negative charges on Bacterial cell walls, and then form pores

Cathelicidins
-Broad spectrum of microbiocidal activity
-Structurally diverse
-disrupts membranes and acts
to activate additional cell types
The two main types of antimicrobial peptides found in humans are the defensins and cathelicidin
Pathogen Associated Molecular Patterns PAMPs

Pattern Recognition Receptors (PRRs)
Example:
Toll receptors

spaetzle – follicle cells secrete into ventral side of oocyte
Wild type                                                    Toll
The name of the gene family derives from Christiane Nüsslein-Volhard's 1985 exclamation, "Das ist ja toll!“ The exclamation, which translates as "That's amazing!" was in reference to the underdeveloped ventral portion of a fruit fly larva. The adjective “toll” is German for "amazing" or "great".
Toll

Pattern Recognition Receptors (PRRs)
There are many
Toll-like receptors
Toll like receptors initiate signal transduction cascade which yields multiple important outcomes

Pathogen associated molecular patterns (PAMPS)
Pattern recognition receptors (PRRS)
Trigger for innate immune response
Innate immune response effectors

Innate immunity
Adaptive or acquired immunity

Mucopolysaccharides
Inflammation, Edema
Extravasation

Glands secrete anti-microbial proteins

Antimicrobial proteins are often carried in the cytosolic granules of leukocytes and platets, whereas extracellular agents are either the product of cellular degranulation or secretion from the liver into acute-phase plasma.
Cross section through the sole of the foot
b-defensins are evolutionarily related
b-defensins have similar but not identical functions

Defensins
-synthesized by granulocytes
-cationic- Bind to negative charges on Bacterial cell walls, and then form pores
a-defensins are small peptides 29-35 residues with hallmark six cysteine motif.
b-defensins differ by having up to 45 residues different cysteines but are related.  Also called HBDs, for human b-defensins
There are many proteins with anti-microbial activity
Neutrophils
Degranulation- discharge of the contents of cytoplasmic granules
Neutrophil Granules
